extended-capabilities.step1.li1: gh copilot suggest — Translate a natural-language task description into a shell command
extended-capabilities.step1.li2: gh copilot explain — Get a plain-language explanation of any shell command
extended-capabilities.step1.li3: GitHub CLI (gh) v2.x or later installed and authenticated — Install from cli.github.com
extended-capabilities.step1.li4: A GitHub Copilot license — Individual, Business, or Enterprise. Learn more about plans
extended-capabilities.step1.li5: A supported shell — Bash, Zsh, Fish, or PowerShell
extended-capabilities.step1.p1: GitHub Copilot CLI brings AI-powered assistance directly to your terminal, helping you craft shell commands, explain what commands do, and navigate your development environment more efficiently.
extended-capabilities.step1.p2: Copilot CLI is a GitHub CLI extension that lets you ask Copilot questions in plain language directly from your terminal. It supports two key sub-commands:
extended-capabilities.step1.p3: This topic has a dedicated hands-on workshop page with step-by-step exercises covering installation, command suggestions, explanations, shell aliases, a real-world challenge, and tips & best practices.
extended-capabilities.step1.p4: Install the extension and start using it in minutes:

extended-capabilities.step2.li10: Run /mcp again to confirm the github server is connected.
extended-capabilities.step2.li11: Now try a prompt that uses it: List my open pull requests in this repository and summarize what each one changes.

extended-capabilities.step2.li15: Watch how Copilot calls the server's tools rather than guessing at file contents.
extended-capabilities.step2.li16: In VS Code, open the Command Palette and run MCP: Add Server (or edit .vscode/mcp.json).
extended-capabilities.step2.li17: Add the same github or filesystem server definition you used in the CLI.
extended-capabilities.step2.li18: Open Copilot Chat in Agent mode and confirm the new tools appear in the tools picker.

extended-capabilities.step2.li2: Resources — Read-only data the model can pull in for context (files, records, API responses)
extended-capabilities.step2.li20: Find the three most recently updated issues assigned to me and draft a status update.
extended-capabilities.step2.li21: Read package.json with the filesystem server, then check the GitHub repo for any open Dependabot PRs that match those dependencies.
extended-capabilities.step2.li22: Create a new GitHub issue titled "Flaky login test" describing the failing test in tests/login.spec.ts.
extended-capabilities.step2.li23: Which MCP tool would you use to do X, and why? Walk me through the call before you run it.
extended-capabilities.step2.li24: Find an existing MCP server for it (databases, issue trackers, search, cloud providers, and more are available in the community registry).
extended-capabilities.step2.li25: Add it to your CLI config with the correct transport and credentials.
extended-capabilities.step2.li26: Write three prompts that chain its tools together with your repository context.
extended-capabilities.step2.li27: Note which tool calls Copilot chose and whether it asked for approval before acting.
extended-capabilities.step2.li28: Least privilege — Give each server only the credentials and scopes it truly needs
extended-capabilities.step2.li29: Review tools before trusting — Inspect a server's exposed tools with /mcp before letting the model call them
extended-capabilities.step2.li3: Prompts — Reusable, parameterized prompt templates the server offers
extended-capabilities.step2.li30: Prefer official servers — Use first-party or well-maintained servers for anything touching production data
extended-capabilities.step2.li31: Keep secrets in env vars — Reference credentials via environment variables, never hard-coded in config
extended-capabilities.step2.li32: Start local — Test with a local stdio server before exposing remote HTTP servers to a team

extended-capabilities.step2.li36: MCP in VS Code — Configuring MCP servers for Copilot agent mode
extended-capabilities.step2.li4: Start the CLI by running copilot in your terminal.
extended-capabilities.step2.li5: Type /mcp and press Enter to list configured MCP servers and their status.
extended-capabilities.step2.li6: Note which servers are connected and expand one to see the tools it exposes.
extended-capabilities.step2.li7: Ask Copilot: What MCP tools do you have available right now? and compare its answer to the /mcp list.
extended-capabilities.step2.li8: In the CLI, run /mcp add and follow the prompts (or edit your MCP config file directly — see below).
extended-capabilities.step2.li9: Configure it as an HTTP server pointing at the hosted GitHub MCP endpoint, or run it locally. A typical JSON config entry looks like this:
extended-capabilities.step2.p1: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI tools like GitHub Copilot connect to external data sources and tools through a common interface. Instead of building a one-off integration for every service, you point Copilot at an MCP server and it instantly gains new tools, resources, and context.
extended-capabilities.step2.p10: MCP is a shared standard, so the servers you configured also work in VS Code's Copilot agent mode.
extended-capabilities.step2.p11: Once you have a couple of servers connected, experiment with prompts that combine them with your codebase:
extended-capabilities.step2.p12: Reach for MCP when you want Copilot to take real actions against a system (query a database, file an issue, hit an internal API) instead of just reasoning about text. If a capability already exists as an MCP server, configuring it is almost always faster than building a custom integration.
extended-capabilities.step2.p13: Pick a system you use daily and connect it through MCP:
extended-capabilities.step2.p2: Think of MCP as a "USB-C port for AI" — a single, standardized way to plug capabilities into an AI agent. An MCP server exposes tools (actions the model can call), resources (data the model can read), and prompts. An MCP client (such as the Copilot CLI or VS Code) discovers those capabilities and makes them available to the model during a conversation.
extended-capabilities.step2.p3: The diagram below shows how a Copilot client talks to one or more MCP servers, each of which fronts a real system:
extended-capabilities.step2.p4: For the hands-on exercises you'll need the GitHub Copilot CLI installed and authenticated, plus Node.js 18+ (so npx can launch community MCP servers). VS Code with the Copilot extension is optional but useful for Exercise 4.
extended-capabilities.step2.p5: MCP servers communicate over one of two transports. You'll pick one when configuring a server:
extended-capabilities.step2.p6: The Copilot CLI manages MCP servers with the /mcp slash command. Let's see what's already wired up.
extended-capabilities.step2.p7: The GitHub MCP server gives the model first-class tools for issues, pull requests, and repositories. Add it to the CLI:
extended-capabilities.step2.p8: Never paste a personal access token directly into a config file you commit. Use an environment variable (e.g. ${GITHUB_PAT}) and grant the token only the scopes the server needs. Review which tools a server exposes before approving it.
extended-capabilities.step2.p9: Local servers are launched as subprocesses. Let's add the community filesystem server so the model can read and write files in a directory you choose.
extended-capabilities.step2.strong3: When to add an MCP server
extended-capabilities.step2.td1: Runs as a local subprocess on your machine
extended-capabilities.step2.td2: Runs as a hosted service reached over the network
extended-capabilities.step2.td3: Great for local tools (filesystem, git, databases)
extended-capabilities.step2.td4: Great for shared, team-wide, or SaaS integrations
extended-capabilities.step2.td6: Configured with a URL and auth headers

extended-capabilities.step3.li10: Browse skills.sh and download the find-skills skill into .github/copilot/skills/, then ask Copilot to find skills for "generate API documentation" and read through the matches.
extended-capabilities.step3.li11: Decide whether an existing skill fits your need — if one does, note which one and why.
extended-capabilities.step3.li12: If nothing fits, download the create-skill skill from skills.sh and ask Copilot to scaffold a new api-docs skill.
extended-capabilities.step3.li13: Open the generated SKILL.md, fill in the description and applyTo patterns, and add one concrete example of the output you expect.
extended-capabilities.step3.li14: Invoke the skill on a real file and compare the result against doing the same task without the skill.
extended-capabilities.step3.li15: Be specific — Provide concrete examples and clear guidelines rather than vague instructions
extended-capabilities.step3.li16: Keep it focused — Each skill should address one specific task or workflow
extended-capabilities.step3.li17: Use code examples — Show Copilot the exact patterns and style you expect
extended-capabilities.step3.li18: Scope appropriately — Use applyTo patterns to ensure skills are invoked at the right time
extended-capabilities.step3.li19: Document conventions — Include your team's specific coding standards and patterns

extended-capabilities.step3.li20: Version control — Check skills into your repository so the whole team benefits

extended-capabilities.step3.li67: Sample skills in your repository at .github/copilot/skills/
extended-capabilities.step3.li8: find-skills — Searches the available skill catalog (built-in and community skills) for ones relevant to your task, so you can reuse an existing skill instead of reinventing it.
extended-capabilities.step3.li9: create-skill — Scaffolds a brand-new skill for you: it generates the SKILL.md file with sensible front matter (name, description, applyTo) and a structured body, ready for you to refine.
extended-capabilities.step3.p1: Agent Skills in VS Code Copilot Chat allow you to define custom, reusable behaviors that guide how Copilot responds to specific types of requests. Skills are defined using markdown files that Copilot discovers and invokes automatically based on the context of your conversation.
extended-capabilities.step3.p10: Writing skill files by hand is great for learning, but you don't have to start from a blank page. skills.sh hosts a catalog of community skills, including two meta-skills — helper skills whose job is to manage other skills — that you can download and drop straight into your .github/copilot/skills/ folder:
extended-capabilities.step3.p11: A typical flow is "look before you build" — download the meta-skills from skills.sh, then search for an existing skill, and only scaffold a new one if nothing fits:
extended-capabilities.step3.p12: Reusing a discovered skill saves time and keeps your team consistent, while create-skill ensures every new skill starts with valid front matter and a predictable structure — fewer typos in applyTo patterns and a faster path to a working skill.
extended-capabilities.step3.p13: Practice the "look before you build" workflow end to end:
extended-capabilities.step3.p14: Create a skill that guides Copilot to review pull requests with specific criteria:
extended-capabilities.step3.p15: Create a skill for generating API documentation in a specific format:
extended-capabilities.step3.p16: Put your skills to work in real scenarios:
extended-capabilities.step3.p17: You can see which skills Copilot is using by checking the VS Code Copilot Chat panel. Skills that are active for the current context will be indicated in the response.
extended-capabilities.step3.p18: Understanding when to use each customization approach:
extended-capabilities.step3.p19: Agent Skills require VS Code with the GitHub Copilot Chat extension. Make sure you have the latest version installed for the best experience.
extended-capabilities.step3.p2: Skills are instructions stored in .SKILL.md files that tell Copilot how to handle specific tasks or workflows. When you ask Copilot a question, it can detect which skills are relevant and use them to provide more tailored, domain-specific responses.
extended-capabilities.step3.p20: Design and implement a skill for an actual workflow in your team's project:
extended-capabilities.step3.p3: Think of skills as specialized tools in Copilot's toolkit — each one teaches Copilot a specific capability, such as:
extended-capabilities.step3.p4: Copilot looks for skill files in specific locations within your project:
extended-capabilities.step3.p5: Skills are automatically loaded when you open a workspace, and Copilot will invoke them based on the conversation context and the skill's applyTo patterns.
extended-capabilities.step3.p6: Let's create a skill for writing unit tests. Create a file named write-tests.SKILL.md in your .github/copilot/skills/ directory:
extended-capabilities.step3.p7: The front matter (between --- lines) defines metadata about the skill, including when it should be applied. The content provides instructions that guide Copilot's behavior.
extended-capabilities.step3.p8: The applyTo field accepts glob patterns that specify which files or contexts should trigger the skill:
extended-capabilities.step3.p9: When you're working with a file that matches these patterns, Copilot will automatically consider this skill when generating responses.

extended-capabilities.step4.caption1: Sequential multi-agent flow: the orchestrator delegates to each sub-agent in turn and merges their output.

extended-capabilities.step4.li1: Separate concerns by having specialized agents for research, implementation, testing, etc.
extended-capabilities.step4.li10: Break it into one todo per file — confirm none of the todos depend on each other.
extended-capabilities.step4.li11: Note how this differs from the sequential research → plan → implement pipeline, where each todo does depend on the previous one.
extended-capabilities.step4.li12: Decide which approach fits: a Fleet (parallel, independent) or sequential sub-agents (ordered, dependent).
extended-capabilities.step4.li13: /fleet Add a standard MIT license header to every source file in the src/ directory — one agent per file.
extended-capabilities.step4.li14: /fleet Write a unit test file for each module in src/services/ that currently has no tests.
extended-capabilities.step4.li15: /fleet For each README in the packages/ folder, fix broken relative links and update the install instructions.
extended-capabilities.step4.li16: /fleet Migrate each of these components from class syntax to function components: Header, Footer, Sidebar, Card.
extended-capabilities.step4.li17: /fleet Review each open file in this folder for accessibility issues and report findings per file.
extended-capabilities.step4.li18: /fleet Generate a one-paragraph summary for each markdown doc in docs/ and collect them into a list.
extended-capabilities.step4.li19: Pick a repetitive, per-file task in your project (e.g. adding a header comment to several files).
extended-capabilities.step4.li2: Reuse agent definitions across different workflows
extended-capabilities.step4.li20: In the CLI, run /fleet and describe it as "one agent per file" so the work fans out.
extended-capabilities.step4.li21: Watch the agents run in parallel, then review each agent's result before accepting the changes.
extended-capabilities.step4.li22: Compare the wall-clock time to doing the same edits sequentially with a single agent.

extended-capabilities.step4.li24: research.agent.md — Specialized agent for code analysis and research
extended-capabilities.step4.li25: implementation.agent.md — Specialized agent for writing code

extended-capabilities.step4.li29: In VS Code Copilot Chat, test your agent by typing: @research analyze the authentication setup in this project
extended-capabilities.step4.li3: Maintain focused context within each specialized agent
extended-capabilities.step4.li30: Observe how the agent follows the instructions you provided
extended-capabilities.step4.li31: Create orchestrator.agent.md that can delegate to your research agent
extended-capabilities.step4.li34: Test the workflow: @orchestrator add a user profile feature to the app
extended-capabilities.step4.li35: Observe how the orchestrator delegates tasks to research first, then implementation
extended-capabilities.step4.li36: When the orchestrator calls the research agent, it passes the user's request
extended-capabilities.step4.li37: The research agent returns structured findings
extended-capabilities.step4.li38: The orchestrator includes those findings when calling the implementation agent
extended-capabilities.step4.li39: The final response combines insights from both agents
extended-capabilities.step4.li4: Build complex multi-step workflows with clear task delegation
extended-capabilities.step4.li40: Research Agent: Analyzes the codebase and gathers requirements
extended-capabilities.step4.li41: Plan Agent: Creates a detailed implementation plan based on research
extended-capabilities.step4.li42: Implementation Agent: Executes the plan and writes the code
extended-capabilities.step4.li43: Separation of Concerns: Each agent has a clear, focused responsibility
extended-capabilities.step4.li44: Reusability: Sub-agents can be reused across different workflows
extended-capabilities.step4.li45: Better Context Management: Specialized agents maintain focused context
extended-capabilities.step4.li46: Modularity: Easy to add or modify specialized agents
extended-capabilities.step4.li47: Quality: Specialized agents often produce higher quality output in their domain
extended-capabilities.step4.li48: Complexity: More moving parts to manage and maintain
extended-capabilities.step4.li49: Latency: Multiple agent calls increase overall response time
extended-capabilities.step4.li5: Orchestrator agent — Receives user requests and decomposes them into sub-tasks
extended-capabilities.step4.li50: Cost: Each agent invocation uses tokens, increasing API costs
extended-capabilities.step4.li51: Coordination Overhead: Orchestrator must manage handoffs and context passing
extended-capabilities.step4.li52: Debugging: Harder to trace issues across multiple agent interactions
extended-capabilities.step4.li53: Complex tasks that benefit from specialized expertise
extended-capabilities.step4.li54: Tasks requiring distinct phases (research → plan → implement)
extended-capabilities.step4.li55: When you want to reuse agent definitions across projects
extended-capabilities.step4.li56: When quality matters more than speed
extended-capabilities.step4.li59: When keeping costs low is a priority
extended-capabilities.step4.li6: Research sub-agent — Gathers information and analyzes the codebase
extended-capabilities.step4.li61: Keep each agent's instructions clear and focused on a single responsibility
extended-capabilities.step4.li62: Document the expected input and output format for each agent
extended-capabilities.step4.li63: Pass context explicitly between agents rather than relying on implicit state
extended-capabilities.step4.li64: Test individual agents before integrating them into workflows
extended-capabilities.step4.li65: Monitor token usage and costs when using multi-agent workflows
extended-capabilities.step4.li66: Version control your agent definitions alongside your code
extended-capabilities.step4.li67: Complete Multi-Agent Examples — Download working agent definitions

extended-capabilities.step4.li69: GitHub Copilot Customization Guide
extended-capabilities.step4.li7: Implementation sub-agent — Writes code and creates files based on research findings
extended-capabilities.step4.li8: Testing sub-agent (optional) — Creates tests for the implemented code
extended-capabilities.step4.li9: Take a task in your project that touches several independent files (for example, adding a license header to every source file).
extended-capabilities.step4.p1: Sub-agents allow you to create specialized Copilot agents that work together in coordinated workflows. An orchestrator agent delegates tasks to specialized sub-agents, enabling modular, multi-step workflows that combine different AI capabilities.
extended-capabilities.step4.p10: For example, if you need to add the same logging call to twelve unrelated modules, or write tests for several independent components, a fleet can assign one agent per item and complete them concurrently — dramatically cutting wall-clock time.
extended-capabilities.step4.p11: Use a fleet when work splits cleanly into independent tasks that can run in any order (e.g. per-file changes, per-component tests, batch migrations). Stick with sequential sub-agents when each step genuinely needs the output of the one before it. A good signal: if your todo list has items with no dependencies between them, those are fleet candidates.
extended-capabilities.step4.p12: In the Copilot CLI, when you finish planning a highly parallelizable set of todos, the CLI can offer to run them as a fleet rather than one by one. Practice recognising the pattern:
extended-capabilities.step4.p13: The Copilot CLI exposes fleets through the /fleet slash command. It lets you launch several agents at once, each on its own independent task, and then review their results together. Start the CLI with copilot, type /fleet, and describe the parallel work you want done.
extended-capabilities.step4.p14: Describe a set of independent units of work and let the fleet fan them out — one agent per unit. The clearer the boundaries between tasks, the better the parallel agents perform. Avoid tasks where one item must finish before another can start.
extended-capabilities.step4.p15: Open a real project and experiment with these. Each one fans out into independent agents:
extended-capabilities.step4.p16: We've created a complete multi-agent example for you to review. Download the example files:
extended-capabilities.step4.p17: Review these files to understand how agents are structured and how they reference each other.
extended-capabilities.step4.p18: Pay attention to how context flows between agents:
extended-capabilities.step4.p19: Experiment by modifying the orchestrator to pass different context or format the output differently.
extended-capabilities.step4.p2: Sub-agents are custom agent definitions (.agent.md files) that are invoked by a parent agent rather than directly by the user. The parent agent acts as an orchestrator, deciding which sub-agent to invoke and how to combine their results into a unified response.
extended-capabilities.step4.p20: Design a complete pipeline with three specialized agents:
extended-capabilities.step4.p21: Create agent definitions for each, set up the orchestrator to call them in sequence, and test with a real feature request.
extended-capabilities.step4.p22: Start with a single agent and only introduce sub-agents when you find that tasks naturally break into distinct phases or require different types of expertise. Premature decomposition can add unnecessary complexity.
extended-capabilities.step4.p3: This differs from single-agent workflows where one agent handles all aspects of a task. With sub-agents, you can:
extended-capabilities.step4.p4: Custom agents are defined in .agent.md files stored in your workspace. These files contain instructions that shape how the agent behaves:
extended-capabilities.step4.p6: The diagram below shows how a request flows from the user through the orchestrator, fans out to specialized sub-agents, and how their results are combined back into a single response:
extended-capabilities.step4.p7: This exercise requires VS Code with the GitHub Copilot extension and Copilot Chat with agent mode enabled. You'll need a GitHub Copilot license to use these features.
extended-capabilities.step4.p8: The orchestrator pattern above runs agents sequentially — research, then plan, then implement — because each step depends on the previous one. But many tasks are made up of independent pieces of work that don't need to wait on each other. For these, the GitHub Copilot CLI offers a Fleet.
extended-capabilities.step4.p9: A Fleet dispatches multiple Copilot agents that run in parallel, each working on a separate, self-contained task at the same time. Instead of one orchestrator handing off context down a chain, a fleet fans out the work and gathers the results when each agent finishes.
extended-capabilities.step4.strong2: When to reach for a Fleet
extended-capabilities.step4.strong3: How to phrase a good fleet prompt
extended-capabilities.step4.td1: One agent at a time; each depends on the last
extended-capabilities.step4.td2: Many agents at once; tasks are independent
extended-capabilities.step4.td3: Context flows down a chain (research → plan → implement)
extended-capabilities.step4.td4: Each agent gets its own focused task and context
extended-capabilities.step4.td5: Best for phased work where order matters
extended-capabilities.step4.td6: Best for parallelizable work where order doesn't matter
extended-capabilities.step4.td7: Total time = sum of all steps
extended-capabilities.step4.td8: Total time ≈ the slowest single task

extended-capabilities.step5.li2: Long-term memory — Persisted facts stored in an external store (database, vector store, etc.) and retrieved on demand
extended-capabilities.step5.li3: Episodic memory — Summaries of past conversations stored and retrieved to inform future interactions
extended-capabilities.step5.li4: At the start of each conversation, retrieve relevant memories based on the user's message
extended-capabilities.step5.li5: Inject the retrieved memories into the system prompt as context
extended-capabilities.step5.li6: At the end of the conversation, extract and store key facts from the exchange
extended-capabilities.step5.li7: Build a simple in-memory store for your agent using a Python list or dictionary
extended-capabilities.step5.li8: Add a remember skill that stores a key-value fact provided by the user
extended-capabilities.step5.li9: Add a recall skill that retrieves stored facts and inject them into the agent's response
extended-capabilities.step5.p1: Copilot Memory enables agents to persist and retrieve information across conversations, allowing for more personalized, context-aware interactions over time.
extended-capabilities.step5.p2: By default, Copilot agents are stateless — each conversation starts fresh. Memory adds a persistence layer that lets agents store facts, preferences, and context that can be recalled in future sessions.
extended-capabilities.step5.p3: A common approach uses a vector database to store and semantically search memories:
extended-capabilities.step5.p4: To integrate memory into a Copilot agent:
extended-capabilities.step5.p5: Always be transparent with users about what information is being stored and provide a way for them to review or delete their stored memories.
extended-capabilities.step5.title: Copilot Memory
